<!--
  TEMPLATE INSTRUCTIONS
  Lines starting with "> **Guidance:**" are instructional prompts.
  Delete them as you fill in each section.
  Sections marked [Required] should always be completed.
  Sections marked [Optional] can be removed if genuinely not applicable — but consider whether they apply before removing.
-->

# ADR-[NNN]: [Title]

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **Status** | Proposed / Accepted / Deprecated / Superseded |
| **Date** | YYYY-MM-DD |
| **Owner(s) / Deciders** | [Names of people who made or are making this decision] |
| **Related RFC** | [Link to the RFC where this decision was discussed, if applicable] |
| **Related Links** | [Links to relevant PRDs, tickets, Slack threads, prior ADRs] |

---

## Decision `[Required]`

> **Guidance:** State the decision in one clear sentence. This is the most important line in the document — a reader who reads nothing else should understand what was decided. Use active voice: "Use X for Y" rather than "It was decided that X would be used for Y."

## Context `[Required]`

> **Guidance:** Describe the situation that required this decision. What problem, requirement, or trigger made it necessary to choose? Include enough context that a future engineer — or AI agent — who has never worked on this system can understand why this decision was needed. Reference the RFC or PRD for detailed background rather than restating it.

## Options Considered `[Required]`

> **Guidance:** List the options that were evaluated. For each, provide a brief description and its key strengths and weaknesses. Be fair to rejected options — understanding why they were viable but not selected is as important as understanding the chosen option.

### Option 1: [Name] (Chosen)

**Description:**

**Pros:**
-

**Cons:**
-

### Option 2: [Name]

**Description:**

**Pros:**
-

**Cons:**
-

### Option 3: [Name]

> **Guidance:** Include as many options as were genuinely evaluated. Two is the minimum; include more if they were seriously considered.

**Description:**

**Pros:**
-

**Cons:**
-

## Rationale `[Required]`

> **Guidance:** Explain why the chosen option was selected over the alternatives. Reference specific decision drivers: requirements it satisfies, constraints it respects, risks it mitigates, trade-offs it optimizes for. This is the section that enables future engineers to evaluate whether the original reasoning still holds when circumstances change. Without rationale, decisions become unchallengeable traditions.

## Consequences `[Required]`

> **Guidance:** Describe what follows from this decision — both positive and negative. Include impacts on architecture, team workflows, performance, cost, operational complexity, developer experience, or future flexibility. Be honest about the costs. Consequences help future engineers understand the full impact of this decision and anticipate where it may create friction.

### Positive

-

### Negative

-

## Risks / Follow-Ups `[Optional]`

> **Guidance:** Identify risks introduced by this decision and follow-up actions needed. For risks, include likelihood and mitigation. For follow-ups, include owners and timelines where known.

| Item | Type | Owner | Status |
|------|------|-------|--------|
| | Risk / Follow-Up | | |

## Supersedes / Superseded By `[Optional]`

> **Guidance:** If this ADR replaces a previous decision, link to the superseded ADR. If this ADR has been superseded by a later decision, link to the new ADR and update this ADR's status to "Superseded." Maintaining these links prevents engineers from following outdated decisions.

| Relationship | ADR |
|-------------|-----|
| Supersedes | |
| Superseded by | |

## Related Documents `[Optional]`

> **Guidance:** Link to related PRDs, RFCs, ADRs, or external resources. Provide brief context for each link.

| Document | Relationship |
|----------|-------------|
| | |
